rationale: The pairing of the blind-men-and-elephant parable with these specific six Old-Javanese-Saiva analogies (head = water jar, ear = winnowing fan, trunk = snake, belly = granary, tail = eel, leg = pillar), the explicit framing it within the question 'Śaiva or Pāśupata or Alepaka?', and the coinage of the technical term andhatattva are all unique to Vrhaspatitattva — none of Tattvajñāna, Mahājñāna, Gaṇapatitattva, the Sanskrit smṛtis, or any other corpus work uses this andhatattva coinage or this six-part Śaiva-Pāśupata-Alepaka frame around the parable.

prompt: Find the legal-procedure chapter in the corpus that names exactly five ordeals — the balance, fire, water, poison, and the sacred libation — and then prescribes their procedural details with these specific numbers: an iron ball of fifty palas placed in the accused's hands, who must walk through seven circles each of sixteen finger-breadths, with seven Aśvattha leaves bound on his hands; for the poison ordeal the accused must eat 'the Śārṅga poison from the Himalayan mountains'; and for the sacred-libation ordeal the accused is pure if no calamity befalls him within fourteen days. Quote the verse that addresses the Balance as 'O Mother' (asking to be carried up if pure and taken down if a sinner), and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The closed pentad balance/fire/water/poison/kosa with these exact numerical specifications — fifty palas, seven circles of sixteen aṅgulas, seven Aśvattha leaves, Śārṅga poison from the Himalayas, fourteen-day kosa wait — and especially the addressing of the Balance as 'O Mother' (mātar) is a signature passage of Yājñavalkya's vyavahārādhyāya. Nārada-smṛti, Bṛhaspati-smṛti, and Kātyāyana-smṛti also discuss ordeals, but none combines this specific set of procedural numbers with the maternal address to the Balance, and Manu only mentions ordeals in passing without these details.

prompt: Find the legal text in the corpus that lays down a graduated monthly interest schedule with this exact dual structure: when there is a pledge, the lawful interest is one-eightieth of the principal per month; otherwise, it is two, three, four, or five percent according to the order of the four varṇas; and that further allows ten percent for those who travel through forests and twenty percent for those who travel by sea. The same passage also caps the maximum recoverable interest at eight times the principal for liquids, four times for cloth, three times for grain, and twice the principal for gold. Quote the verses fixing both the per-varṇa monthly rate and the forest/sea traveller rate, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This precise three-tier interest framework — eightieth-part-per-month with a pledge, 2/3/4/5 percent by varṇa without one, plus the 10 percent / 20 percent forest-traveller / sea-traveller rates, capped at 8x liquids / 4x cloth / 3x grain / 2x gold — is the canonical Yājñavalkya schema and is not reproduced in this exact form in Manu, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Viṣṇu, or the Vīramitrodaya digest. The forest/sea-traveller carve-out at 10% and 20% is especially distinctive.

prompt: Find the work in the corpus that, inside a chapter on expiation, gives a precise anatomical inventory of the human body with these specific numbers: a man's body has three hundred and sixty bones, sixty-four teeth with their sockets, twenty nails, seventy-two ribs (with sockets and nodules), forty-five bones in the back, fifteen in the neck, and seventeen in the chest; the body contains seven hundred veins, nine hundred sinews, two hundred arteries, and five hundred muscles; it has one hundred and seven vital spots and two hundred joints; and the body hairs together with the sweat ducts number 'fifty crores and four crores… and sixty-seven and a half lakhs.' Quote the verse declaring 'three hundred and sixty bones' and the verse giving 'seven hundred veins, nine hundred sinews, two hundred arteries, and five hundred muscles,' and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This itemized anatomical census — 360 bones, 64 teeth, 20 nails, 72 ribs, 700 veins / 900 sinews / 200 arteries / 500 muscles, 107 marmas, 200 joints, and the 54-crore-plus body-hair count — appears in Yājñavalkya's prāyaścitta chapter and is a unique signature. Manu's penance chapter focuses on transmigration tables and bodily disfigurements as karmic signatures, not on a numerical bone-and-vein catalogue; Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, Yama, Viṣṇu, and the Vīramitrodaya digest do not reproduce this specific anatomical inventory.

prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes a fourfold-value fine for a very specific list of grocery adulterations — pepper mixed with stone fragments, salt mixed with earth, and asafoetida mixed with tree resin — placed alongside the related rule that ghee sold under the name of ghee but adulterated with sesame oil (or oil with ghee) likewise incurs a fourfold penalty on the merchant? rationale: The exact triplet 'pepper with stone fragments, salt with earth, asafoetida with tree resin' — paired with the ghee-and-sesame-oil rule and the fourfold-value penalty — is a signature commercial-fraud catalogue unique to this smṛti; no other smṛti in the corpus (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa) names asafoetida-with-resin or pepper-with-stone-fragments as adulteration species, and the word 'asafoetida' appears nowhere else in the corpus.

prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus declares that for the convergence of all sins together no ordinary penance is known, and that the prescribed remedy in such a case is to take up the skull-bowl and observe the Kāpālika vow — explicitly named by the wise as the ultimate penance — even though for each single sin committed alone the wise know a separate penance? rationale: The specific doctrinal pairing — that no penance exists when all sins converge, and that the skull-bowl Kāpālika vow is the explicit ultimate remedy — is unique to this smṛti; the strings 'skull-bowl', 'Kāpālika vow', and 'all sins converge' do not appear in any other corpus work (Manu, Yājñavalkya, Nārada, Bṛhaspati, Viṣṇu, Kātyāyana, Parāśara, Aṅgirasa, Vīramitrodaya).

prompt: Which Sanskrit penitential text in the corpus prescribes that the king should banish from the kingdom a Brahmin who has abandoned his sacred fire by first smearing the man's body with ashes and fumigating him with the hair and bones of a corpse — and, in an immediately adjacent verse, makes 'the man who violates convention' go naked after being fanned with the hair of a corpse and his head adorned with a garland of corpse-hair? rationale: This precise pair of degradation rituals — body smeared with ashes plus fumigation with corpse-hair-and-bones for the abandoner of the sacred fire, then fanning with corpse-hair plus a corpse-hair head-garland for the convention-violator who is sent away naked — is unique to this smṛti; the words 'fanned' and 'fumigated with hair' in this purificatory-banishment sense do not appear in any other corpus smṛti, and no other dharmaśāstra in the collection clusters these specific corpse-hair humiliations together.
